does: This paper identifies a fundamental identifiability problem in standard RLHF where aggregating binary preference labels from heterogeneous anonymous labelers creates systematic distortions in the learned reward model. To solve this, the authors propose augmenting preference datasets with response times and model each labeling decision as a Drift-Diffusion Model (DDM). They introduce a novel consistent estimator that leverages the joint distribution of choices and response times to recover the true population-average preference, proving asymptotic convergence to ground truth even when individual labelers provide only single data points. The method corrects biases that occur with choice-only approaches and enables more accurate alignment of LLMs to genuine aggregate human preferences.
connects: This work builds on the RLHF literature that trains reward models from human feedback, extending recent critiques of standard pooled-preference approaches that ignore labeler heterogeneity. It connects to identifiability theory in econometrics and preference learning, which has shown that choice data alone can lead to unidentifiable or inconsistent estimates when preferences vary across decision-makers. The use of Drift-Diffusion Models draws from cognitive science and psychometrics, where response times have long been recognized as informative signals about underlying decision processes. The paper combines insights from behavioral economics, causal inference, and learning theory to address how auxiliary signals beyond choices can improve inference about latent preferences in crowdsourced settings.
enables: This approach opens directions for more robust preference learning from diverse, anonymous labelers in large-scale alignment settings. It suggests that small additional data collection efforts—recording response times during preference labeling—can substantially improve the quality of reward models without requiring identity tracking or explicit preference heterogeneity modeling. The work points toward richer models of the human labeling process that leverage behavioral signals to disentangle true preferences from noise and individual differences. Future work could explore other auxiliary signals beyond response time, extend the DDM framework to more complex preference structures, or apply these methods to improve alignment not just for average preferences but for capturing meaningful preference distributions across populations.
